food_name: Ondeh Ondeh (pandan glutinous rice, palm sugar)
food_subgroup: Pandan glutinous rice ball with palm sugar filling, rolled in coconut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Ondeh Ondeh. Nearest parent is a glutinous rice confectionery or steamed rice ball. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based confectionery — glutinous rice ball with filling (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Ondeh Ondeh consists of 4 pieces (approximately 25g each, 100g total) of small bright green glutinous rice balls with a liquid gula melaka core that bursts in the mouth, rolled in freshly grated coconut.

nutrient_source.title: Ondeh Ondeh — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11090041
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Foodo under food code F11090041 — Ondeh Ondeh, standard Peranakan preparation with pandan dough and gula melaka filling.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — glutinous rice (cooked); GI very high due to the waxy starch structure of glutinous rice flour and the high sugar content from gula melaka.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 82mg per 4-piece serving (100g) — about 4% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; Ondeh Ondeh is one of the lowest-sodium kueh in Singapore.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume Ondeh Ondeh occasionally; the sodium content is negligible, though the high sugar content warrants attention for overall metabolic health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice flour has a very high GI (~78); the gula melaka filling adds 18.5g sugar per 100g; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 32 from just 4 small pieces is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should strictly limit Ondeh Ondeh consumption; the combination of very high GI glutinous rice and high sugar from palm sugar filling produces a significant postprandial glucose spike; 1–2 pieces maximum as an occasional treat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote inflammation at frequent consumption; pandan provides chlorophyll antioxidants; coconut provides medium-chain saturated fatty acids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Ondeh Ondeh to occasional treats; the high sugar and refined glutinous rice content are pro-inflammatory at frequent consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ondeh Ondeh (Onde Onde) is a beloved Peranakan and Malay kueh (sweet cake) of small glutinous rice flour balls dyed vivid green with fresh pandan juice, filled with a nugget of gula melaka (palm sugar) that dissolves to a liquid when cooked, and rolled in freshly grated coconut. When bitten, the ball releases a burst of caramel-sweet palm sugar juice. They are one of the most visually striking and flavour-forward kueh in Singapore's traditional confectionery repertoire, sold at Peranakan cake stalls, wet markets, and traditional kueh shops.
common_user_queries: how many calories in ondeh ondeh
can a diabetic eat ondeh ondeh
ondeh ondeh vs ang ku kueh calories

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F11090041 — Ondeh Ondeh, standard Peranakan preparation.
notes: Fully vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free in the standard preparation. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. Sugar content from gula melaka is the primary nutritional concern. The coconut coating adds medium-chain saturated fatty acids. Pandan provides trace chlorophyll and phytol antioxidants.
